coredata.dc:title: High acid-base tolerance and long storage time lanthanum cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Bangxing
Wu, Fei
Xie, Zhenjun
Kang, Xing
Wang, Yanghua
Li, Wei
Hu, Xiaolin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, lanthanum and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots (LaCe-CQDs) was firstly synthesized by one step hydrothermal method. The obtained LaCe-CQDs shown sable fluorescence properties with pH values from 3 to 9 and after 4 weeks of storage. The average particle size of LaCe-CQDs, with excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 nm and 446 nm, is 3.27 ± 0.12 nm. Selective analysis of various metal ions revealed the sensitivity of LaCe-CQDs towards Fe3+ ions. Within the 0–60 μM, the fluorescence intensity exhibits a strong linear correlation with the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.753 μM. Additionally, the accuracy of LaCe-CQDs were demonstrated in natural water samples. Therefore, LaCe-CQDs are a promising sensor for Fe3+ detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Lanthanum and cerium co-doped material
Fluorescence probe
Fe3+ iron detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ ions in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of LaCe-CQDs; (b) High-resolution TEM images of LaCe-CQDs; (c) The size distribution of LaCe-CQDs; (d) FI-TR spectra of LaCe-CQDs. Insert of C: The lattice spacing of LaCe-CQDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum analysis of LaCe-CQDs; (b–f) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, La3d and Ce3d for LaCe-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis, PL, and PLE spectra of LaCe-CQDs; (b) PL emission spectra of LaCe-CQDs under different excitation light; (c) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of LaCe-CQDs; (d) Fluorescent lifetime decay curve of LaCe-CQDs;(e) Effect of pH; (f) Shelf-time on fluorescence intensity of LaCe-CQDs.

(a) Selectivity study of LaCe-CQDs toward different ions; (b) Fluorescence intensity of LaCe–CQDs-Fe3+solution after adding different ions; (c) The fluorescence spectra of LaCe-CQDs solutions after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–100 μM); (d) Relationship between Fe3+ concentration in range of 0–60 μM and fluorescence intensity.
(a) Fluorescence lifetime of LaCe-CQDs after adding Fe3+ (b) Ultraviolet absorption spectra of LaCe-CQDs before and after adding Fe3+ (The concentration of Fe3+ is 40 μM).
Comparison the LOD and detection range of different carbon dot- based fluorescent probe for detection of Fe3+ ions.
Quantitative determination of Fe(Ⅲ) in actual water samples.

head.ce:title: High acid-base tolerance and long storage time lanthanum cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots for Fe3+ detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Science, Chongqing University of Technology, Chongqing 400054, China (B. Li).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, lanthanum and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots (LaCe-CQDs) was firstly synthesized by one step hydrothermal method. The obtained LaCe-CQDs shown sable fluorescence properties with pH values from 3 to 9 and after 4 weeks of storage. The average particle size of LaCe-CQDs, with excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 nm and 446 nm, is 3.27 ± 0.12 nm. Selective analysis of various metal ions revealed the sensitivity of LaCe-CQDs towards Fe3+ ions. Within the 0–60 μM, the fluorescence intensity exhibits a strong linear correlation with the concentration of Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.753 μM. Additionally, the accuracy of LaCe-CQDs were demonstrated in natural water samples. Therefore, LaCe-CQDs are a promising sensor for Fe3+ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is an essential trace element in living organisms, playing an important role in maintaining living systems. Iron deficiency can result in anemia [1], Parkinson's disease [2] and even mental health issues [3]. However, excessive intake of iron ions can cause damage to the heart, liver and other vital organs of the human body, potentially leading to cirrhosis, cancer and various diseases [4]. Simultaneously, the accumulation of iron in the soil and water environment significantly impacts the normal growth of plants and animals [5]. Currently, various methods such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [6], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [7] and electrochemical method [8] are available. However, these techniques often necessitate extensive sample pretreatment and incur high costs. Therefore, it is necessary to explore simpler and cheaper iron ion detection methods.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional nanometer material firstly discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. [9] in the process of preparing single-wall carbon nanotubes by electrophoresis. The characteristics of good water solubility, stable optical properties, good resistance to photobleaching, and easy functionalization are possessed by carbon quantum dots [10]. The core of carbon quantum dots is mainly graphitized sp2 hybridized carbon, and the surface is rich in –COOH, –OH and other groups [9,11]. The main synthesis methods of carbon quantum dots include hydrothermal method [12], electrochemical method [13], microwave thermal method [14], etc. Among them, hydrothermal method is the most simple and convenient method, which can be prepared in one step without complicated control. Studies have demonstrated that the fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots can be improved by heteroatom doping effectively, which provides the possibility of interacting with specific metal ions by changing the electronic structure related to the CQDs bandgap [15,16]. In recent years, the emerging rare earth doped carbon dots (RE-CDs) have attracted much attention because they combine the advantages of rare earth and CDs [17], with rare earth metal ions having larger atomic radii, more electrons, and unoccupied empty orbitals than most metal atoms [18].

In this work, we use citric acid and urea as precursors, lanthanum nitrate and cerium nitrate as dopants, and use a one-step hydrothermal method to prepare lanthanum and cerium co-doped carbon quantum dots (LaCe-CQDs). The prepared carbon quantum dots emit blue light under 350 nm excitation, and the emission peak is at 446 nm. The stability of LaCe-CQDs is demonstrated by PH values and storage time. The LaCe-CQDs has been applied to a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection. The probe has good selectivity and stability for Fe3+ in the range of 0-60μMand the detection limit (LOD) is 0.753 μM. In addition, the LaCe-CQDs is also very stable in the detection of various real water samples.

Citric acid monohydrate, urea, lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (LaN3O9·6H2O), cerium nitrate hexahydrate (CeN3O9·6H2O) were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), chromium chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7H2O) and manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (China). Dialysis bag was obtained from Hunan Qicheng Material Technology Co., Ltd (Changsha, China).

First, 0.84 g citric acid, 0.36 g urea, 0.866 g lanthanum nitrate and 0.868 g cerium nitrate were dissolved in 15 ml deionized water, stirred at room temperature for 30 min with a magnetic agitator, and the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor with 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene for 10 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was centrifuged (8000rm, 15 min), and then the supernatant was put into a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for dialysis for 1d, and the water was changed every 6 h. Finally, the sample was freeze-dried for 36 h to obtain LaCe-CQDs powder, which was stored at 4 °C. In addition, La-CQDs and Ce-CQDs doped with single lanthanum and single cerium were prepared by the same method without the addition of lanthanum and cerium nitrate.

A series of selective ions with a concentration of 1 mmol/L, such as Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+, have been prepared and added into LaCe-CQDs solution for selective testing. Fe3+ solutions with different concentrations (0-80μΜ) were prepared, and LaCe-CQDs solution was added for sensitivity test.

In addition, the stability of LaCe-CQDs solution at different PH values (2–13) was also studied. The fluorescence spectra of each sample (λex = 350 nm, λem = 446 nm) were determined, and the blank sample was set as F0.

The standard for quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 solvent) resulted in st value of 0.54 at 360 nm. The QY formula is exemplified in Eq. (1) [19,20]:
(1)
Φ =
Φ
st
×
I
I
st
×
A
st
A
×
η
2
η
st
2

In formula (1), Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, I is the integral area of the emission intensity in the fluorescence spectrum, A is the absorbance intensity, η is the refractive index of the sample. The subscript 'st' indicates reference to quinidine sulfate. To minimize the reabsorption effect, the absorbance intensity of all tested samples must be kept below 0.1.

Transmission electron microscope and high-resolution transmission electron microscope were utilized to observe the morphology of LaCe-CQDs. The instrument used was JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope of JEOL Company. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was measured by Thermo Scientific's K-Alpha; Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured by PerkinElmer's Spectra 2; The fluorescence lifetime was measured on the steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer FLS980. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum was measured by UV-3600plus ultraviolet–visible photometer (Shimadzu). Fluorescence spectra were performed on the FL-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water, mineral water and lake water of Chongqing University of Technology have been collected to verify the detection effect of LaCe-CQDs in real water samples. The feasibility and practicability of fluorescent probes have been verified by adding Fe3+ ions of different concentrations and calculating the recovery rate of ions.
The collected lake water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove impurities and insoluble particles.

In order to explore the optimal synthesis ratio of LaCe-CQDs, La-CQDs with different proportions of citric acid and urea have been prepared, as shown in Fig. S1. The results show that when the ratio of citric acid to urea is 1:3, the prepared La-CQDs has the highest fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S2, the influence of different lanthanum-cerium doping ratios on LaCe-CQDs is explored. The results show that when the lanthanum-cerium ratio is 1:1, the prepared LaCe-CQDs has the highest fluorescence intensity. Fig. S3 reflects the comparison of fluorescence intensity of La-CQDs, Ce-CQDs and LaCe-CQDs, and it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of LaCe-CQDs is higher than that of La-CQDs. It is proved that lanthanum-cerium co-doping can improve the fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots. There is a synergistic effect between lanthanum and cerium to improve the fluorescence properties of carbon quantum dots. Fig. 1 shows the preparation process of LaCe-CQDs and the application of Fe3+ sensing. Transmission electron microscopy was used to analyze the structure and morphology of LaCe-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), LaCe-CQDs is uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution and spherical. The HRTEM images (Fig. 2b) show a lattice spacing of 0.211 nm for LaCe-CQDs, corresponding to the (100) face of graphene (see illustration in Fig. 2b) [21]. For the size calculation of 50 samples, the particle size distribution of LaCe-CQDs ranges from 1.5-5.5 nm, with an average diameter of 3.27 ± 0.12 nm (Fig. 2 c). Fig. S4 shows the XRD pattern of LaCe-CQDs, in which spikes related to La and Ce can be observed, indicating that the crystal structure of CQDs has changed [22]. The functional groups on the surface of LaCe-CQDs are determined by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2(d)). The wide absorption peak at 3310 cm−1 corresponds to the tensile vibration of OH and NH bond [23], and the absorption peak at 2370 cm−1 corresponds to the tensile vibration of CN bond. The absorption peaks at 1631 cm−1 and 659 cm−1 belong to the tensile vibration of CO and LaH bond respectively [24].

The XPS full spectrum of LaCe-CQDs is shown in Fig. 3 (a). Five absorption peaks can be observed at 285.40 eV, 400.69 eV, 531.83 eV, 837.52 eV and 885.26 eV, which belong to C1s, N1s, O1s, La3d and Ce3d, respectively. XPS data show that carbon elements accounts for 50.04 %, oxygen elements for 41.85 %, nitrogen elements for 2.29 %, lanthanum elements for 2.92 % and cerium elements for 2.9 % in LaCe-CQDs. Fig. 3(b) is the high-resolution spectrum of C1s, in which three absorption peaks of 284.79 eV, 286.53 eV, and 288.4 eV correspond to CC, CO, and CO groups respectively [25]. Fig. 3(c) is the high-resolution spectrum of N1s. The absorption peaks at 399.80 eV and 401.49 eV correspond to pyrrole nitrogen and graphite nitrogen groups, respectively [26]. Fig. 3(d) shows the high-resolution spectra of O1s. The absorption peaks at 531.27 eV, 532.31 eV and 533.98 eV correspond to CH, CO and COC/COH groups [27], respectively. Fig. 3(e) shows the high-resolution spectrum of La3d. The absorption peaks of 835.18 eV and 838.32 eV correspond to La = O and LA-H, respectively, while the absorption peaks of 851.91 eV and 855.14 eV belong to LA-O [28]. Fig. 3(f) is the high-resolution spectrum of Ce3d, from which it can be seen that there is a mixture of trivalent and quadrivalent Ce in LaCe-CQDs [29]. There are six absorption peaks in the Ce3d curve, among which the absorption peaks at 885.10 eV and 903.80 eV belong to Ce (Ⅲ), while those at 882.10 eV, 887.07 eV, 900.65 eV and 903.80 eV belong to Ce (Ⅳ) [30]. FT-IR spectra and XPS spectra show that there are rich functional groups such as carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of LaCe-CQDs, and La and Ce elements are successfully doped in it [31].

The prepared LaCe-CQDs are light brown under natural light and blue under ultraviolet light, as shown in the illustration in Fig. 4 (a). The optical properties of LaCe-CQDs are analyzed through UV–VIS absorption spectrum and photoluminescence spectrum. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the UV–VIS absorption spectrum of LaCe-CQDs has two absorption peaks in the range of 200–500 nm. The strong absorption peak at 215 nm is caused by the π-π ∗ transition of the aromatic compound CC [32], while the weak absorption peak at 337 nm is caused by the n → π* transition of the aromatic compound CO [33]. The maximum excitation wavelength of LaCe-CQDs is 350 nm and the maximum emission wavelength is 446 nm. Fig. 4(b) shows that as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 nm to 390 nm, the emission wavelength of LaCe-CQDs also shows a redshift phenomenon, which indicates that LaCe-CQDs has a good excitation wavelength dependence. The reason for this special property is that there are various groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots, they generate new surface and edge states in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [34]. Fig. 4(c) shows the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of LaCe-CQDs, where the X-axis represents the emission wavelength and the Y-axis represents the excitation wavelength, which proves that changing the excitation wavelength does not produce a new luminescence center, but only changes the fluorescence intensity around the luminescence center [35]. Fig. 4(d) shows the fluorescence lifetime of LaCe-CQDs measured (excitation at 320 nm). The decay data can be well fitted by a two-exponential equation, the average fluorescence lifetime of Lace-CQDs can be calculated by
τ =
(
A 1
τ
1
2
+
A 2
τ
2
2
)
/
(
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
)
, the results show that the τ of the LaCe-CQDs is 3.541 ns (τ1 = 2.037 ns, τ2 = 8.099 ns), and the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.999. In addition, the fluorescence stability of LaCe-CQDs was verified through a series of tests, as shown in Fig. 4(e), the fluorescence stability of LaCe-CQDs under different PH values was explored. The synthesized LaCe-CQDs remained stable between PH = 3 and PH = 9, and the fluorescence intensity was the highest when PH = 9. The fluorescence intensity will be affected in over-acid and over-basic environments. Over-basic environment can inhibit the emission of –COOH functionalization, and over-acid environment can also reduce fluorescence by protonating amines to aggregate LaCe-CQDs [36]. Fig. 4(f) shows the change of fluorescence intensity of LaCe-CQDs after four weeks of placement. It can be seen that LaCe-CQDs still maintains a high fluorescence intensity after four weeks of placement, which proves that the fluorescence emission is stable for a long time. As shown in Fig. S5, a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiated LaCe-CQDs for 90 min, and the fluorescence intensity decreased with the ultraviolet irradiation time. After irradiation for 90 min, the fluorescence intensity decreased to 75 %. In summary, LaCe-CQDs has good luminous performance and fluorescence stability.

In order to evaluate the selectivity capability of LaCe-CQDs for Na+, K+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+ and Fe2+ were prepared aqueous solution at PH = 7 for testing. The fluorescence quenching of different ions is shown in Fig. 5 (a), where F denote the fluorescence intensity after adding different ions (1 mmol/L), and F0 denote the fluorescence intensity without adding metal ions LaCe-CQDs. The results show that after adding Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of LaCe-CQDs has significantly quenched by 8.5 % of that without Fe3+. The quenching effect of other ions for LaCe-CQDs is much smaller than that of Fe3+. The addition of Fe3+ changes the surface charge distribution of LaCe-CQDs, causing the excited electrons of LaCe-CQDs are transfer to the three-dimensional empty orbital of Fe3+, forming a non-radiative complex [37]. In order to further verify the anti-interference capability of LaCe-CQDs for Fe3+ detection, a series of interfering ions are added to LaCe-CQDs-Fe3+ solution, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The results show that the quenching degree after the addition of interfering ions is consistent with that of blank samples. This suggests that the effect of interfering with the detection of ion pairs is negligible. Fig. 5(c) shows the fluorescence intensity spectra of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–100 μM) added to LaCe-CQDs solution. The fluorescence intensity of LaCe-CQDs gradually decreased with the concentration of Fe3+ increased. There is a good linear relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0–60 μM, as shown in Fig. 5(d). The fitting equation is Y = 0.44519X + 0.10341 (R2 = 0.987). According to the principle of three standard deviations, the limit of detection (LOD) can be calculated by LOD = 3σ/k. Where σ is the standard deviation of LaCe-CQDs and k is the slope of the calibration curve [38,39]. The calculated Lod of LaCe-CQDs is 0.753 μM. The results indicate that LaCe-CQDs has good selectivity and low detection limit for Fe3+. Table 1 lists the comparison of this work with published work related to the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions based on carbon dots.

There are several reasons for the quenching of quantum dots and quenchers: static quenching, dynamic quenching, internal filtration effect, energy transfer and photoinduced electron transfer [44]. Dynamic quenching refers to the effect of energy transfer or charge transfer caused by the collision between LaCe-CQDs and the quencher, which leads to the weakening of fluorescence and the shortening of fluorescence lifetime [45]. Static quenching means that LaCe-CQDs interacts with the quencher to form a non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent material, while its excited state lifetime hardly changes [11]. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the fluorescence lifetime of LaCe-CQDs after adding 40μΜ Fe3+ is 3.537 ns, while that before adding is 3.541 ns. It can be seen that the fluorescence lifetime of LaCe-CQDs does not change with the adding of Fe3+, which indicates that the quenching process may be caused by static quenching. In addition, the UV–visible absorption spectra of LaCe-CQDs before and after the adding of Fe3+ can also be proved. As can be seen from Fig. 6(b), after the adding of Fe3+, the absorption peak of LaCe-CQDs at 225–350 nm is significantly enhanced, which indicates that new substances are generated on the surface, which conforms to the principle of static quenching. Fig. S6 illustrates the quenching efficiency of LaCe-CQDs at 15 ℃, 25 ℃, 35 ℃ and 45 ℃ after the introduction of 15 μM Fe3+. The F0/F value exhibits a gradual decrease with increasing temperature, decreasing from 2.358 to 2.098. This is because the generated coordination compound will decompose when the temperature rises [46], which also proves that the LaCe-CQDs quenching is caused by static quenching.

The detection of metal ions in actual water samples is very important for human health, and the state stipulates that the content of iron ions in drinking water cannot be higher than 0.3 g/L. Tap water, mineral water and still lake water have been used to verify the feasibility of LaCe-CQDs detection in actual water samples. The test results are shown in Table 2 . 5,10 and 20 μM Fe3+ are added to test water samples, respectively, and the actual recovery rates of water samples range from 95.43 % to 105.40 %. This proves that LaCe-CQDs has a high accuracy and recovery rate in actual water samples, and is expected to be applied to the Fe3+ detection of actual water samples.

In summary, a sensitive and simple LaCe-CQDs fluorescence sensor using citric acid and urea as precursors and lanthanum nitrate and cerium nitrate as doping substances is prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared LaCe-CQDs has good fluorescence properties and fluorescence stability, and emits blue light (446 nm) when excited at 350 nm. The stability at different PH, time of placement, etc. is also discussed. The application of LaCe-CQDs is prepared with Fe3+ sensing, and the fluorescence of LaCe-CQDs can be selectively quenched after Fe3+ is added. In the range of 0–60 μΜ, the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration, and the detection limit was 0.753 μM. LaCe-CQDs has good linear range, sensitivity and tolerance for Fe3+ detection, and low detection limit. It is determined that the reaction between LaCe-CQDs and Fe3+ is static quenching. In addition, the detection recovery rate of LaCe-CQDs for Fe3+ in actual water samples is between 95.43 % and 105.40 %, which proves that LaCe-CQDs has the potential to detect Fe3+ in actual water samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau (cstc2024ycjh-bgzxm0038, cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0923), Project of 2023 science and technology research program of Chongqing Education Commission of China (KJQN202304407) and Organization Department of Chongqing Municipal Committee (Chongqing Talent innovation and entrepreneurship demonstration team, CQYC20220309959).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125403.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
